quality.rationale: 多步矩阵代数与双曲函数推导结合Wigner旋转物理图像

origin.question: Problem 1.26. Two successive, arbitrary pure Lorentz boosts $\\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_1$ and $\\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_2$ are equivalent to a pure boost $\\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_3$ followed by a pure rotation $\\theta \\underline{\\mathbf{n}}$ , where $\\mathbf{n}$ is a unit vector. Find the magnitude of $\\theta$ in terms of $\\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_1$ and $\\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_2$ and show that $\\underline{\\mathbf{n}} \\cdot \\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_3 = 0$ .
origin.answer: $\\tan\\left(\\frac{1}{2}\\theta\\right)=\\frac{-\\sin h\\left(\\frac{1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\\right)\\sin h\\left(\\frac{1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\\right)\\sin\\gamma}{\\cos h\\left(\\frac{1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\\right)\\cos h\\left(\\frac{1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\\right)+\\cos\\gamma\\sin h\\left(\\frac{1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\\right)\\sin h\\left(\\frac{1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\\right)}$, 且 $\\underline{\\mathbf{n}} \\cdot \\underline{\\mathbf{v}}_3 = 0$
origin.solution:
Solution 1.26. The hard way to solve the problem is by multiplying $4 \times 4$ matrices together and then decomposing into rotations and boosts. An easier way is to use the $2 \times 2$ complex unimodular matrix representation of the Lorentz group developed in Problem 1.25. Represent a pure boost of velocity $\mathbf{v}_1$ by
$$
L \left(\underline {{v}}_{1}\right) = \exp \left(- v_{1}^{*} \underline {{n}}_{1} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}} / 2\right) = \cos h \left(v_{1}^{*} / 2\right) + \left(\underline {{n}}_{1} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}\right) \sin h \left(v_{1}^{*} / 2\right) \tag {1a}
$$
where $\underline{\mathfrak{n}}_1$ is a unit vector in the direction of the boost and $\mathbf{v}_1^*$ parametrizes the magnitude of the boost. Represent a pure rotation $	heta$ by
$$
\mathrm {L} \left(\underline {{\theta}}_{1}\right) = \exp \left(\mathrm {i} \theta^{*} \underline {{\mathbf {n}}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}} / 2\right) = \cos \left(\theta^{*} / 2\right) - \mathrm {i} \left(\underline {{\mathbf {n}}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}\right) \sin \left(\theta^{*} / 2\right) \tag {1b}
$$
where $\mathfrak{n}$ is the rotation axis and $	heta^{*}$ parametrizes the magnitude of the rotation.
For pure boosts in the same direction, $\mathbf{v}^{*}$ is in fact the rapidity parameter, $\mathbf{v}^{*} = \tan h^{-1}|\mathbf{v}|$ , since $\mathbf{v}^{*}$ adds linearly, e.g.
$$
L \left(v_{1}\right) L \left(v_{2}\right) = e^{- v_{1}^{*} \sigma_{x} / 2} e^{- v_{2}^{*} \sigma_{x} / 2} = e^{- \left(v_{1}^{*} + v_{2}^{*}\right) \sigma_{x} / 2}.
$$
Likewise one can see that for pure rotations in the same direction, $	heta^{*}$ is just the magnitude of the rotation angle, $	heta^{*} = |\underline{\theta}|$ .
Using Equations (1a) and (1b) one now solves the relation
$$
\mathrm {L} (\underline {{\mathrm {v}}}_{1}) \mathrm {L} (\underline {{\mathrm {v}}}_{2}) = \mathrm {L} (\theta \underline {{\mathrm {n}}}) \mathrm {L} (\underline {{\mathrm {v}}}_{3}) \tag {2}
$$
for $	heta, \underline{\mathfrak{n}}, \underline{\mathfrak{v}}_3$ in terms of $\underline{\mathbf{v}}_1$ and $\underline{\mathbf{v}}_2$ . A useful identity in multiplying the right hand sides of Equation (2) together is
$$
(\underset {\sim} {\mathrm {A}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}) (\underset {\sim} {\mathrm {B}} \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}) = \mathrm {A} \cdot \mathrm {B} + \mathrm {i} (\underset {\sim} {\mathrm {A}} \times \underset {\sim} {\mathrm {B}}) \cdot \underline {{\sigma}}.
$$
Since the $\underline{\sigma}$ and identity $2 \times 2$ matrix are all independent, one may equate, in Equation (2), the real and imaginary parts respectively of terms multiplying the identity matrix and of terms multiplying the $\sigma$ matrices, yielding two scalar and two vector equations:
$$
\begin{array}{l} \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) + \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) \left(\underline {{n}}_{1} \cdot \underline {{n}}_{2}\right) (3a) \\ = \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{3}^{*}\right) \cos \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta^{*}\right) \\ \sin \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{3}^{*}\right) \left(\underline {{n}} \cdot \underline {{n}}_{3}\right) = 0 (3b) \\ \end{array}
$$
$$
\begin{array}{l} \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) \underline {{n}}_{2} + \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) \underline {{n}}_{1} = \cos \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{3}^{*}\right) \underline {{n}}_{3} \\ + \sin \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{3}^{*}\right) \left(\underline {{n}} \times \underline {{n}}_{3}\right) \tag {3c} \\ \end{array}
$$
$$
\sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) \left(\underline {{n}}_{1} \times \underline {{n}}_{2}\right) = - \sin \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta^{*}\right) \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{3}^{*}\right) \underline {n}. \tag {3d}
$$
In the general case, the solution to Equation (3b) is
$$
\underline {{\underline {{\mathbf {n}}}}} \cdot \underline {{\underline {{\mathbf {n}}}}}_{3} = 0 \tag {4}
$$
i.e., the resultant rotation is orthogonal to the resultant boost.
Let $\gamma$ be the angle between $\underline{\mathfrak{n}}_1$ and $\underline{\mathfrak{n}}_2$ . Then, dotting $\underline{\mathfrak{n}}$ into Equation (3d) and combining with Equation (3a), one finds
$$
\tan \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta^{*}\right) = \frac {- \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) \sin \gamma}{\cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \cos h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right) + \cos \gamma \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{1}^{*}\right) \sin h \left(\frac {1}{2} v_{2}^{*}\right)} \cdot \tag {5}
$$
Note that $	heta (= 	heta^{*}$ by above discussion) may take on all values $0 \leq \theta \leq 2\pi$ except $	heta = \pi$ , and that for $\mathfrak{n}_1 = \mathfrak{n}_2 (\gamma = 0)$ , $	heta = 0$ and there is no net rotation as expected.
